text.title: Evaluation Metrics and Benchmarks
text.summary: Automated metrics by task type, standard benchmarks with saturation warnings, and LLM-as-judge best practices.

rule.statement: Select 2–3 primary metrics per task type and define success thresholds before running any evaluation.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc metric selection is p-hacking. Thresholds defined after seeing results are uninformative.

list.item: Classification: Accuracy, F1, Precision, Recall; secondary: Confusion matrix
Generation: BLEU, ROUGE-L, BERTScore; note: BLEU/ROUGE correlate poorly with human preference for open-ended generation
QA: Exact match, Contains match, F1
Code: pass@1, pass@10; secondary: Syntax validity

section.title: Standard Benchmarks (2025-2026)
section.p: Use benchmarks appropriate for your domain; avoid over-reporting MMLU or HumanEval scores for production comparisons — both are saturating for top models.

list.item: Knowledge/Reasoning: MMLU-Pro (harder, expert-reviewed), GPQA (validated difficulty), ARC
Code: LiveCodeBench (contamination-free), SWE-bench (real GitHub issues), HumanEval+
Safety: ToxiGen, BBQ (9 social categories), RealToxicityPrompts

antipattern.description: Reporting MMLU or HumanEval as primary production quality signal.
antipattern.reason: Both benchmarks are saturating for frontier models — score differences are compressed and may not reflect production task performance. Use domain-specific test sets from real queries instead.

antipattern.description: Using the same model for both generation and LLM-as-judge evaluation.
antipattern.reason: Self-evaluation is systematically biased toward the model's own outputs. Always use a different judge model.

rule.statement: Use multiple judge models (GPT-4 + Claude) and average scores to reduce evaluator bias; report confidence intervals, not single-point estimates.
rule.rationale: GPT-4 prefers GPT-4 outputs; Claude prefers Claude outputs. Multi-judge averaging partially cancels bias. Single-point estimates hide variance that matters for production decisions.

list.item: Choose judge model: GPT-4 or Claude Sonnet as default judges
Provide clear rubrics with 0–3 or 0–5 scales per criterion
Run multiple judgments per output for consistency check
